transcript.presenting_concern: Ambivalence about sobriety — exploring decisional balance

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Carlos, thanks for coming back. How has the week been?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's been... I don't know. Complicated. I've been thinking a lot about what we discussed last time — the "pros and cons" thing. The decisional balance. It's been messing with my head a little.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: In what way?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I always thought of my drinking as no big deal. Everyone drinks. But when I wrote out the cons list like you asked... it was longer than I expected. And seeing it on paper hit different than just vaguely knowing it in my head.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What stood out to you on the cons list?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The thing about my daughter. I wrote "Elena doesn't invite her friends over anymore because she's afraid I'll be drinking." My wife told me that. She's eleven. Her dad's drinking is embarrassing enough that she doesn't want her friends to see. That gutted me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's painful to sit with. An eleven-year-old making social decisions based on her father's drinking. How did you feel when you wrote that down?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Ashamed. And angry at myself. And then — honestly — I wanted a drink to not feel that. Which is kind of the whole problem, right? Feel bad → drink → feel worse → drink more.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You just named the maintenance cycle perfectly. The drinking temporarily numbs uncomfortable feelings, but it creates new uncomfortable feelings — shame, health consequences, relationship damage — which then drive more drinking. It's self-reinforcing.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: But here's the thing — I also had stuff on the pros list that felt just as real. "Drinking helps me unwind after work." "It's how I socialize — every guy I know drinks." "I don't know who I am at a barbecue without a beer in my hand."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Those are real too. I'm not going to dismiss them. The relaxation function, the social bonding, the identity piece — those are genuine benefits you experience from drinking. The question isn't whether those benefits exist. It's whether they're worth the costs. And whether there are other ways to get those same benefits without the costs.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's where I get stuck. Because in theory, sure — I could relax without drinking. Exercise, whatever. But in practice, after a twelve-hour shift at the plant, a beer hits different than a jog.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I hear you. And I'm not here to tell you what to do. This is your life and your choice. What I can do is help you look at it clearly and decide what YOU want. So let me ask this: when you imagine your life a year from now with your drinking staying exactly as it is — same amount, same pattern — what do you see?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] I see Elena being twelve and still not bringing friends home. I see my wife getting more distant — she's already sleeping in the guest room some nights when I've had too many. I see my blood pressure going up — the doctor warned me last month. I see... honestly, I see it getting worse, not better. It only goes one direction if I don't change something.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And if you imagine a year from now where something HAS changed — you don't have to define exactly what yet — what does that look like?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Elena's friends are at the house. My wife is next to me on the couch again. I'm coaching Elena's soccer team — she asked me to but I said no because Saturday mornings after Friday night drinks are rough. I see myself being more... present. Like actually there instead of half-absent.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That second image — the present version of yourself with Elena's friends in the house and you coaching soccer — on a scale of 1-10, how important is that to you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nine. Ten. It's everything. My family is everything.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And how confident are you — 1-10 — that you could make changes to move toward that image?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Honestly? A four. Because I've tried to cut back before and it lasts like two weeks and then I'm back to normal.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So importance is a nine or ten but confidence is a four. That gap is where we work. Let me ask — you said you've tried to cut back before. What's happened in those attempts?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'll say "I'm only having two beers a night." That works for a week or so. Then there's a stressful day, or my buddy comes over, or there's a game on, and two becomes four becomes six and I'm back where I started. I feel like I don't have a middle gear. It's either total control or none.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That pattern — difficulty moderating, all-or-nothing — is really common. Some people can moderate and some find that moderation is harder than abstinence because the first drink lowers their ability to stick to limits. What do you think about that for yourself?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I think... and I hate saying this... I think I might not be a moderate guy. Two beers might not be possible for me. Which means the alternative is...

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You tell me. What's the alternative?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Zero. None. Which terrifies me because I don't know how to be Carlos without drinking. I've been drinking since I was sixteen. That's thirty-two years.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a significant identity concern. "Who am I without this?" It makes sense that it's scary. You're not just changing a behavior — you're reimagining who you are in social situations, at work events, at family gatherings. That's big.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So what do I do? I'm not ready to say "I'll never drink again." That feels impossible.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Then don't say it. Nobody's asking you to commit to forever right now. What if we worked with shorter time frames? What would feel manageable?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I could probably do a week. Seven days. I've done that before — when I was sick once, I didn't drink for five days and the world didn't end.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What would you learn from a seven-day experiment?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Whether I can actually do it when I'm not sick. How hard it really is. How I feel without it. Whether my wife notices.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Those are all valuable data points. It's not a commitment to lifelong sobriety — it's an experiment to gather information. What would make that week easier?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Telling my wife. If she knows, she can help. And maybe not having beer in the house — if it's not there, I can't grab one on autopilot.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Both smart strategies — social support and environmental control. When would you start?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Tomorrow? If I say next week I'll find an excuse to delay.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I respect that. Starting tomorrow. Seven days. No alcohol. Tell your wife tonight. Remove what's in the house. What will you do instead when the after-work urge hits?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe the gym? My buddy has a membership he's been trying to get me to use. Or I could take Elena to the park. She's always asking me to play catch.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Either of those would serve the relaxation function AND build connection — with your friend or with Elena. You're not losing something — you're replacing it with something better aligned with your values.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: When you put it that way... yeah. Okay. Seven days starting tomorrow. I'll report back next session.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I'll be curious to hear what you learn. And Carlos — the fact that you're sitting here, doing this hard thinking, being honest about the scary parts — that takes guts. The confidence number will go up as you accumulate evidence that you can do this.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thanks, doc. I'll see you next week. Hopefully sober for seven days.

goals: Ambivalence about sobriety — exploring decisional balance
